Dear Ms. Kearney:
In response to your letter dated July
11,
1577,
I have
fauna no prohibition in the Elections Code or Political Reform
Act against a political committee receiving dcnated articles
for use in fund-raising raffles or sales.
The major question appears to be how the donation and the
money received for selling or taffling the donated item will
be reported by your political committee under the Pulitical
Reform Act of
1974, which
I understand that you have a copy ofl
There is the possibility that they may be counted to some extent
as contributions to the comittee under Government Code Section
82015 and associated regulations
I suggest that you cantact the
Fair Political Practices Commission at P.O.
Box 807,
Sacramento,
California 95804,
as the agency rtsponsible for administration
and implementation of the Pclitical Refcm Act,
for a detailed
answer.
I have forwarded a copy a: your letter for their infor-
maticn.
Very truly yours,
WILLIAM N.
DURLEY
Assistant to the Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform
RICHARD By
MAN'ESS
Legal counsel
Elections and Political Reform

Dear Mr. Monroe:
Thank you for your patience in answering my many
questions by letter and telephone.
I ask your indulgence
again for the following question:
Several people have offered to donata Afghans
or quilts for us to rattle off — sell . or whatever we
may do according to law.
We would like to sell tickets
and have the tickets read:
“Donation".
new we use the raffle system by selling tickets and
then drawing the attachment to the ticket that would have
a number on it or madness of the purchaser?
The Committee
did not know whether the "raffle" system is legal for us
to use.
If this question comes under another department
would you please ancwer as far as your department is con-
cerned, and also have the other department write to us so
that I may have it in writing?
We will, or course, report
any donations on the rows to be filed with your department.
Also, would you please send me:
1.
Copy or the amendments 01' the Political Reform Act
which gives legislation effective January 1, 1977.01ated Dec.8. 1976)
2.
State or California lnxormation Manual on Campaign
Disclosure Provisions o: the Political Retom Act tor 1976.
Thank you for your kind attention to my request.
jig/Baum yours,
smelt/es”
Volunteer Secretary

sections.conclusion: There is no prohibition under the Political Reform Act concerning selling or raffling donated items. There is, however, a reporting obligation. The item must be reported when it is received by the Committee. In this case, the fair market value of the quilt or item must be reported as an in-kind (non-monetary) contribution to your campaign committee. Any tickets sold to the fund raiser must be reported as contributions.
sections.question_synthetic: May a political committee use a raffle or ticket system to sell donated items (afghans/quilts) as a fundraising mechanism, and how should such donations and proceeds be reported under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit raffling donated items; however, the fair market value of donated items must be reported as in-kind contributions and ticket sale proceeds must be reported as contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains three related letters: original inquiry from Ms. Kearney (July 11, 1977), Secretary of State response (August 4, 1977) referring to FPPC, and FPPC response from Technical Assistance Program Coordinator. OCR quality is moderate with some scanning artifacts. Main FPPC advice letter clearly identifies Q/C structure.

QUESTION: s by letter and telephone.
I ask your indulgence
again for the following question:
Several people have offered to donata Afghans
or quilts for us to rattle off — sell . or whatever we
may do according to law.
We would like to sell tickets
and have the tickets read:
“Donation".
new we use the raffle system by selling tickets and
then drawing the attachment to the ticket that would have
a number on it or madness of the purchaser?
The Committee
did not know whether the "raffle" system is legal for us
to use.
If this question comes under another department
would you please ancwer as far as your department is con-
cerned, and also have the other department write to us so
that I may have it in writing?
We will, or course, report
any donations on the rows to be filed with your department.
Also, would you please send me:
1.
Copy or the amendments 01' the Political Reform Act
which gives legislation effective January 1, 1977.01ated Dec.8. 1976)
2.
State or California lnxormation Manual on Campaign
Disclosure Provisions o: the Political Retom Act tor 1976.
Thank you for your kind attention to my request.
jig/Baum yours,
smelt/es”
Volunteer Secretary

CONCLUSION: There is no prohibition under the Political Reform Act concerning selling or raffling donated items. There is, however, a reporting obligation. The item must be reported when it is received by the Committee. In this case, the fair market value of the quilt or item must be reported as an in-kind (non-monetary) contribution to your campaign committee. Any tickets sold to the fund raiser must be reported as contributions.

embedding.summary: In response to a committee's inquiry about raffling donated quilts/afghans for fundraising, the FPPC confirms no prohibition exists but establishes reporting requirements: donated items must be reported at fair market value as in-kind contributions, and ticket sale proceeds must be reported as contributions.
